claims.claim_text: Training/serving skew is asserted to be the number one cause of ML production failures.
claims.span_hint: line 28 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The number one cause of ML production failures isn't bad models—it's **training/serving skew**.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A common cause of ML production failures is **training/serving skew**.
claims.rationale: This is an unsupported rank-order prevalence claim about all ML production failures, so the absolute ranking should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Netflix, Uber, and Airbnb are all said to have built feature stores after learning this lesson the hard way.
claims.span_hint: line 30 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Netflix, Uber, and Airbnb all built feature stores after learning this lesson the hard way.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large ML organizations have built internal feature platforms to reduce feature inconsistency and improve reuse.
claims.rationale: This bundles named-company historical claims without a citable source for the exact trio and framing, so the specifics should be collapsed to the general lesson.

claims.claim_text: Uber built Michelangelo primarily to solve feature consistency, and 30% of ML debugging time was spent on feature issues.
claims.anchor_text: - **Uber built Michelangelo** (their ML platform) primarily to solve the feature consistency problem—they found 30% of ML debugging time was spent on feature issues
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Uber built Michelangelo** as an internal ML platform to standardize model development and serving workflows.
claims.rationale: Uber's ML platform is real, but the stated primary motivation and 30% debugging figure are not backed here by a primary source.

claims.claim_text: Feature computation is said to take 80% of ML pipeline time and receive only 20% of the attention.
claims.anchor_text: - **Feature computation often takes 80% of ML pipeline time**—yet gets 20% of the attention. Feature stores flip this ratio by making feature engineering reusable
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Feature engineering and data preparation can dominate ML system effort**, which is why reusable feature pipelines matter.
claims.rationale: This uses a precise 80/20 split without a direct source, so the unsupported numbers should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The module says Uber coined the term feature store in 2017 and that earlier equivalents existed at Google and Facebook.
claims.anchor_text: - **The term "feature store" was coined by Uber in 2017**, but the concept existed earlier as "feature engineering platforms" at Google and Facebook
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Modern ML platforms popularized the idea of a feature store in the late 2010s**, although similar internal feature platforms existed earlier.
claims.rationale: The sentence mixes a specific historical attribution with additional earlier-company claims that are not jointly supported by a single primary source here.

claims.claim_text: Point-in-time correctness is described as the hardest feature-store problem to solve.
claims.anchor_text: - **Point-in-time correctness** (avoiding data leakage) is the hardest feature store problem to solve—get it wrong and your backtesting lies to you
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Point-in-time correctness** is one of the core feature-store problems to solve; get it wrong and your offline evaluation can be misleading.
claims.rationale: The superlative 'hardest' is an unsupported comparative claim, but the underlying lesson about leakage and misleading evaluation is sound.

claims.claim_text: The module presents an unnamed financial-services credit-risk incident with a 90-day vs 30-day feature bug.
claims.span_hint: line 103 | section: War Story: The $10M Feature Bug
claims.anchor_text: A financial services company deployed a credit risk model. The training pipeline computed "average balance over 90 days" correctly. The serving pipeline had a bug—it computed 30-day average instead.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A credit risk model can fail when the training pipeline computes a feature over one time window and the serving pipeline computes a different one.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed named-domain war story with concrete scenario details, so the false-specific anecdote should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: The war story claims the bug caused $10M in defaults within six months.
claims.span_hint: line 105 | section: War Story: The $10M Feature Bug
claims.anchor_text: The model underestimated risk. They approved loans they shouldn't have. Six months later: $10M in defaults traced to one feature computation bug.
claims.suggested_rewrite: That kind of mismatch can materially distort risk estimates and lead to real financial losses.
claims.rationale: This is fake-precise quantitative incident framing with no source, so the dollar amount and timeline must be removed.

claims.claim_text: The module says a feature store would have prevented the incident entirely.
claims.span_hint: line 107 | section: War Story: The $10M Feature Bug
claims.anchor_text: A feature store would have prevented this entirely.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A feature store could reduce the risk of this class of bug by centralizing feature definitions.
claims.rationale: The absolute prevention claim is too strong for an unsupported anecdote and should be reduced to a defensible causal lesson.

claims.claim_text: Point-in-time correctness is described as the most critical feature-store capability.
claims.anchor_text: The most critical feature store capability is **point-in-time correctness**—ensuring you only use data that was available at prediction time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A core feature store capability is **point-in-time correctness**—ensuring you only use data that was available at prediction time.
claims.rationale: The superlative 'most critical' is an unsupported ranking judgment, while the underlying definition is sound.

claims.claim_text: Feast is an open-source feature store that supports training/serving workflows and point-in-time correct feature retrieval.
claims.rationale: The Feast GitHub README explicitly describes Feast as an open-source feature store with offline and low-latency online serving plus point-in-time correct feature sets.

claims.claim_text: Feast is said to have limited streaming capabilities.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Streaming support exists, but you should verify whether its maturity and operational model fit your use case.
claims.rationale: Current Feast upstream materials document streaming support, so the blanket limitation as written is too stale and unspecific to keep.

claims.claim_text: Tecton is a commercial feature store focused on streaming and real-time ML at scale.
claims.rationale: Tecton's official docs directly describe it as the feature store for real-time ML at scale with real-time and streaming feature computation, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Hopsworks is an open-core platform with feature-store-centric end-to-end ML integration.
claims.rationale: Hopsworks' official docs describe a modular MLOps platform with a standalone feature store plus model registry, serving, vector DB, and data-science platform capabilities, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Databricks Feature Store is a commercial offering integrated with Spark-centric Databricks workflows.
claims.rationale: Databricks' docs cover feature tables, Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines, lineage, and training workflows, but docs.databricks.com is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store integrates with AWS workflows through online and offline stores for real-time inference and historical training.
claims.rationale: AWS documentation explicitly describes SageMaker Feature Store's online and offline stores, real-time low-latency reads, historical training use, and feature discovery within SageMaker workflows.

claims.claim_text: Vertex AI Feature Store is integrated with Google Cloud and supports managed online serving plus feature search and metadata discovery.
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's Vertex AI Feature Store overview describes it as a managed cloud-native Vertex AI service with online serving, offline history in BigQuery, and integrated metadata search/discovery.

further_reading.title: Feast GitHub Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the primary upstream reference for Feast architecture, point-in-time correctness, online serving, and current capability scope.

further_reading.title: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store Concepts
further_reading.why_relevant: It clearly explains online versus offline stores, latest-versus-historical records, and the single-source-of-truth model for feature data.

further_reading.title: About Vertex AI Feature Store
further_reading.why_relevant: It provides a current managed-feature-store reference covering online serving, offline history, BigQuery-backed architecture, and metadata search.

further_reading.title: Monitor Models for Training-Serving Skew with Vertex AI
further_reading.why_relevant: It is a strong primary explainer for why training-serving skew matters operationally and how it shows up in production ML systems.

notes: No shared citation pool appears to exist for this module, so `section_pool_ref` is null and `source_ids` are empty. I skipped Mermaid diagrams, code fences, quiz items and answers, and hands-on exercise steps per instructions. I treated the vendor-comparison table rows as claim-bearing because they assert concrete product capabilities rather than purely pedagogical structure.
